questions.question:
A smart city integrates all modes of transport, uses clean energy and promotes sustainable use of resources. It also uses technology to ensure safety and security of the city, something which critics argue, will lead to a surveillance state. Which of the following can be logically inferred from the above paragraph?

i. All smart cities encourage the use of renewable energy.
ii. Smart cities will have a surveillance system.
iii. Sustainability and surveillance go hand in hand in a smart city.
iv. There is a perception that smart cities promote surveillance.

A. (i) only  B. (ii) and (iii) only  C. (iv) only  D. (i) only
questions.to_find: Which statement can be logically inferred from the given paragraph about smart cities

understand.plain: The paragraph describes a smart city and notes that critics argue its technology-driven safety measures could lead to a surveillance state. We need to identify which statement is a valid logical inference. A logical inference follows necessarily from what is stated — not what is implied speculatively.

keywords.explain: A conclusion that follows necessarily and directly from the given statements without adding extra assumptions.
keywords.example: If all dogs bark and Rex is a dog, we can infer Rex barks — this is a valid inference.

keywords.explain: A government or city that uses extensive monitoring systems to observe its citizens, which critics see as a threat to privacy.
keywords.example: A city that uses CCTV on every street corner to track movement could be called a surveillance state.

keywords.explain: A belief or viewpoint held by some people, which may or may not be objectively true.
keywords.example: Critics perceive smart city technology as surveillance — this is their view, not an established fact.

understand.visual_alt: A paragraph describing smart city features and critics' concerns about surveillance.

given.aim: Identify the logically valid inference from the paragraph about smart cities

terms.meaning: Integrates all transport modes, uses clean energy, promotes sustainable resource use, uses technology for safety and security.
terms.example: A city with electric buses, solar panels, and smart sensors fits this description.
terms.connects: These features are stated facts in the paragraph.

terms.meaning: Critics argue that the technology aspect will lead to a surveillance state — this is a perception, not a stated fact.
terms.example: Privacy advocates might argue that facial recognition cameras in smart cities amount to mass surveillance.
terms.connects: This establishes that surveillance is a perceived outcome, not a confirmed one.

terms.meaning: The paragraph says 'clean energy' — this does not necessarily mean 'renewable energy' exclusively.
terms.example: Nuclear energy is clean but not renewable; equating the two would be an invalid inference.
terms.connects: Statement (i) adds 'renewable' which is not in the paragraph, making it invalid.

given.plan: Check each statement against what is strictly stated in the paragraph. Reject any that add information beyond what the text says. Accept only the one that is a direct and necessary consequence.

steps.title: Evaluate statement (i): All smart cities encourage renewable energy
steps.formula_raw: F logically implies E iff E is true in every assignment where F is true
steps.apply: The paragraph says 'clean energy', not 'renewable energy'. Clean energy can include nuclear, which is not renewable. Statement (i) introduces a term not in the text — it is NOT a valid inference.

steps.title: Evaluate statement (ii): Smart cities will have a surveillance system
steps.formula_raw: F logically implies E iff E is true in every assignment where F is true
steps.apply: The paragraph says critics argue this will lead to surveillance — it is not stated as a certainty. The phrase 'will lead to' is the critics' speculation, not an established fact. Statement (ii) presents it as certain — NOT a valid inference.
steps.note: Confuses a speculative concern with a definitive outcome.

steps.title: Evaluate statement (iii): Sustainability and surveillance go hand in hand
steps.formula_raw: An argument is valid iff there is no assignment making all premises true and C false
steps.apply: The paragraph mentions both sustainability and surveillance separately. There is no statement that they are necessarily linked or go together. Statement (iii) draws an unsupported causal link — NOT a valid inference.
steps.note: Correlation mentioned in the same paragraph does not imply causation or logical dependence.

steps.title: Evaluate statement (iv): There is a perception that smart cities promote surveillance
steps.formula_raw: F logically implies E iff E is true in every assignment where F is true
steps.apply: The paragraph explicitly states 'critics argue' that smart city technology will lead to a surveillance state. Critics arguing is exactly a perception. Statement (iv) correctly captures this as a perception held by critics — this IS a valid inference.
steps.note: The phrase 'critics argue' directly establishes a perception, making (iv) the only valid logical inference.

solution.result: Statement (iv) only — there is a perception that smart cities promote surveillance — is the valid logical inference. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Logical inference rules are applied to distinguish facts stated in the passage from speculative or externally added claims.

questions.question:
Two very famous sportsmen Mark and Steve happened to be brothers and played for country A. Mark teased James, an opponent from country B: "There is no way you are good enough to play for your country." James replied, "Maybe not, but at least I am the best player in my own family." Which one of the following can be inferred from this conversation?

A. Mark was not as good as Steve  B. Steve was not as good as Mark  C. James and Steve were great friends  D. James played better than Steve
questions.answer: B. Steve was not as good as Mark
questions.to_find: What can be inferred from James's reply about Mark and Steve

understand.plain: Mark insults James by saying James is not good enough to play for country B. James's clever comeback is that although he may not be good enough for country B, he is still the best player in his own family. Since James is from country B and Mark is from country A, James is implying he is better than both Mark and Steve, suggesting Steve is not as good as Mark.

keywords.explain: A statement in conversation that hints at something without stating it explicitly.
keywords.example: Saying 'I passed the exam without studying' implies the exam was easy or you are smart.

keywords.explain: James claims to be the best player in Mark's family (which includes Mark and Steve).
keywords.example: If James is better than both Mark and Steve, and Mark insulted James, then Mark is actually worse than someone he disparaged.

keywords.explain: James's comeback 'best in my own family' refers to Mark's family (Mark and Steve), implying he is better than both.
keywords.example: If a rival says 'at least I beat your whole team', you infer everyone on your team lost to them.

understand.visual_alt: A conversation between Mark (country A) and James (country B) about sportsmanship and family talent.

given.aim: Identify what James's reply logically implies about the relative abilities of Mark and Steve

terms.meaning: Brothers from country A, both famous sportsmen who play for country A.
terms.example: Both are professionals good enough for country A's national team.
terms.connects: Mark's insult shows confidence in his own country's standard.

terms.meaning: A sportsman from country B who faces Mark's taunt.
terms.example: James is being told he is not good enough for country B.
terms.connects: James responds by claiming superiority within Mark's family unit.

terms.term: Best player in my own family
terms.meaning: James claims to be better than both Mark and Steve (the only players in Mark's family mentioned).
terms.example: If James > Mark and James > Steve, but Mark insulted James, the irony is that Mark is not even the best in his family — wait, the claim is James is better than both, meaning Steve < James and also Mark < James.
terms.connects: If James is the best in Mark's family, it means James beats both Mark and Steve. Mark insulted James, but Mark himself is worse than James, and so is Steve.

given.plan: Parse James's reply carefully. 'Best player in my own family' — James is from country B, Mark and Steve are from country A (the family). James claims to be the best among Mark, Steve, and himself. Since Mark plays for country A (a high standard), and James beats him, the implication about Steve depends on ordering.

steps.title: Parse the insult structure
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and (B -> C), conclude (A -> C)
steps.apply: Mark says James is not good enough to play for country B. This implies Mark thinks he (and Steve) are better than James.
steps.note: Mark's premise: James < standard for country B.

steps.title: Decode James's comeback
steps.formula_raw: from P or Q and not P conclude Q
steps.apply: James says 'at least I am the best player in my own family.' Here 'my own family' refers to Mark's family (Mark and Steve, who are the brothers under discussion). James is saying: James > Mark AND James > Steve. This means Mark is not the best — and since Mark's family contains only Mark and Steve, and James claims to beat both, Steve must be ranked below James too.
steps.note: The key insight: James's 'family' in this context refers to Mark and Steve's family, not James's blood relatives.

steps.title: Draw the inference about Steve vs Mark
steps.formula_raw: F logically implies E iff E is true in every assignment where F is true
steps.apply: James is claiming he (James) is better than both Mark and Steve. If James > Mark > Steve (a possible ordering), then Steve is not as good as Mark. The specific inference supported is: Steve was not as good as Mark, because Mark managed to make the national team for country A and consider himself better, yet James — who beats all in Mark's family — still says Steve is below James. The comparison of Steve vs Mark: if James beats the 'best' of that family (Mark, who insulted James), Steve who is lesser is certainly below Mark.
steps.note: Option B — Steve was not as good as Mark — is the valid inference. Options A, C, D are not supported by the text.

solution.result: From James's reply, we infer that Steve was not as good as Mark. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Verbal reasoning inference rules help decode the logical structure embedded in a conversational exchange.

questions.question:
Since the last one year, after a 125 basis point reduction in repo rate by the Reserve Bank of India, banking institutions have been making a demand to reduce interest rates on small saving schemes. Finally, the government announced yesterday a reduction in interest rates on small saving schemes to bring them on par with fixed deposit interest rates. Which one of the following statements can be inferred from the given passage?

A. Whenever the Reserve Bank reduces the repo rate, the interest rates on small saving schemes are also reduced.
B. Interest rates on small saving schemes are always maintained on par with fixed deposit interest rates.
C. The government sometimes takes into consideration the demands of banking institutions before reducing the interest rates on small saving schemes.
D. A reduction in interest rates on small saving schemes follows only after a reduction in repo rate by the Reserve Bank of India.
questions.answer: C. The government sometimes takes into consideration the demands of banking institutions before reducing the interest rates on small saving schemes.
questions.to_find: Which statement is a valid inference from the passage about repo rate and small saving schemes

understand.plain: The passage describes a specific sequence of events: RBI reduced repo rate by 125 basis points, then banks demanded small saving scheme rate cuts, and then the government reduced those rates. We must infer only what is directly supported by this one instance — not a universal rule.

keywords.explain: The rate at which the Reserve Bank of India lends money to commercial banks; reducing it makes borrowing cheaper.
keywords.example: If the repo rate drops from 6.5% to 5.25%, banks can borrow cheaper and ideally offer lower loan rates.

keywords.explain: Government-backed savings instruments like PPF, NSC, post office deposits with set interest rates.
keywords.example: PPF and Sukanya Samridhi accounts are small saving schemes offering fixed annual returns.

keywords.explain: One basis point = 0.01%; 125 basis points = 1.25% reduction in the repo rate.
keywords.example: A reduction from 6.5% to 5.25% represents a 125 basis point cut.

understand.visual_alt: A timeline: RBI cuts repo rate by 125bps -> banks demand small saving rate cuts -> government reduces small saving rates.

given.aim: Identify the single valid logical inference from a specific sequence of events described in the passage

terms.meaning: The passage describes one specific instance. We cannot generalize to 'always' or 'whenever' from a single case.
terms.example: Just because it rained after one particular storm warning doesn't mean every storm warning causes rain.
terms.connects: Options A, B, D all make universal claims ('whenever', 'always', 'only after') — these go beyond a single instance.

terms.meaning: After the RBI repo rate cut, banking institutions demanded that small saving rates also be reduced (to make deposits more competitive).
terms.example: If savings accounts pay more than fixed deposits, customers prefer savings accounts, hurting bank business.
terms.connects: The government ultimately reduced rates — this shows it considered the banking demand at least once.

terms.meaning: The events happened in order: (1) repo cut, (2) bank demands, (3) government action.
terms.example: This sequence shows a causal chain in this one instance, but not necessarily a universal rule.
terms.connects: We can infer 'sometimes' government considers bank demands — not 'always' or 'only when'.

given.plan: Test each option: Options A and D use 'whenever' and 'only after' (universal causal claims) — one instance cannot support these. Option B says 'always maintained on par' — the passage shows they are brought on par once, not always. Option C says 'sometimes' — justified by this one instance.

steps.title: Test option A: Whenever RBI reduces repo rate, small saving rates are also reduced
steps.formula_raw: An argument is valid iff there is no assignment making all premises true and C false
steps.apply: The passage gives one instance. 'Whenever' implies every time. A single case cannot establish a universal rule. Option A is NOT a valid inference.

steps.title: Test option B: Interest rates on small saving schemes are always on par with fixed deposit rates
steps.formula_raw: F logically implies E iff E is true in every assignment where F is true
steps.apply: The passage says the government reduced rates 'to bring them on par' — implying they were NOT on par before this action. 'Always' is contradicted by the passage itself. Option B is NOT valid.
steps.note: The word 'to bring' implies they were not on par previously.

steps.title: Test option C: Government sometimes considers banking institution demands
steps.formula_raw: F logically implies E iff E is true in every assignment where F is true
steps.apply: The passage clearly shows: banks demanded a cut, and the government then reduced rates. This one documented instance is enough to support 'sometimes'. Option C is a VALID inference.
steps.note: 'Sometimes' is a weak claim supported by even a single confirmed instance.

steps.title: Test option D: Small saving rate reduction follows ONLY after RBI repo rate cut
steps.formula_raw: An argument is valid iff there is no assignment making all premises true and C false
steps.apply: 'Only after' is an exclusive condition. The passage only describes one scenario where repo rate was cut first. We cannot conclude that a repo cut is the only trigger for small saving rate cuts. Option D is NOT a valid inference.
steps.note: Exclusivity ('only') cannot be inferred from a single example.

solution.result: Option C is the only valid inference: the government sometimes takes banking institution demands into consideration. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Distinguishing between 'sometimes' (existential, provable by one instance) and 'always/whenever/only' (universal, requiring all instances) is the key logical principle applied here.

questions.question:
Under a certain legal system, prisoners are allowed to make one statement. If their statement turns out to be true then they are hanged. If the statement turns out to be false then they are shot. One prisoner made a statement and the judge had no option but to set him free. Which one of the following could be the prisoner's statement?

A. "I will be shot"  B. "I will be set free"  C. "I committed the crime"  D. "You committed the crime"
questions.to_find: Which prisoner statement creates a logical paradox forcing the judge to set the prisoner free

understand.plain: This is a classic logical self-referential paradox. The prisoner must say something that cannot be classified as either true or false without contradiction. If every statement leads to hanging (if true) or shooting (if false), the prisoner needs a statement where both consequences are impossible simultaneously, leaving the judge with no valid action.

keywords.explain: A statement that refers to its own truth value in a way that creates a contradiction.
keywords.example: The liar's paradox: 'This statement is false' — if true, it's false; if false, it's true.

keywords.explain: A situation where assuming either truth value of a proposition leads to the opposite conclusion.
keywords.example: If 'I will be shot' is true, you should be shot; but then the statement is true, so you should be hanged — contradiction.

keywords.explain: When a statement cannot be assigned a definite truth value (true or false) without inconsistency, it forces an indeterminate outcome.
keywords.example: A paradoxical statement creates a loop where neither punishment applies, leaving the authority unable to act.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='200' viewBox='0 0 520 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='520' height='200' rx='10' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><rect x='10' y='10' width='500' height='50' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='260' y='42' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Statement: "I will be shot"</text><rect x='10' y='75' width='230' height='55' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='125' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>If TRUE:</text><text x='125' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Prisoner should be HANGED</text><text x='125' y='138' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>(But was shot? Contradiction!)</text><rect x='280' y='75' width='230' height='55' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='395' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>If FALSE:</text><text x='395' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Prisoner should be SHOT</text><text x='395' y='138' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>(But then TRUE! Contradiction!)</text><rect x='160' y='148' width='200' height='40' rx='8' style='fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);'/><text x='260' y='173' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' style='fill:#fff;'>Judge sets free!</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing that 'I will be shot' creates a logical paradox in both the true and false cases, forcing the judge to set the prisoner free.

given.aim: Find the prisoner's statement that creates an unresolvable contradiction, forcing the judge to set the prisoner free

terms.meaning: If what the prisoner says turns out to be true, the punishment is hanging.
terms.example: If prisoner says 'It is raining' and it is raining, they are hanged.
terms.connects: Any verifiable true statement leads to hanging.

terms.meaning: If what the prisoner says turns out to be false, the punishment is being shot.
terms.example: If prisoner says 'It is raining' and it is not raining, they are shot.
terms.connects: Any verifiable false statement leads to being shot.

terms.meaning: A statement where assuming truth leads to a contradiction and assuming falsehood also leads to a contradiction — so neither punishment can be applied.
terms.example: 'I will be shot' — if true, I should be shot, but that makes the statement true, so I should be hanged; if false, I should not be shot, but then I am hanged, making the statement false again.
terms.connects: Only a self-referential paradoxical statement can force the judge's hand.

given.plan: Test each option under both the 'statement is true' and 'statement is false' scenarios. The correct answer is the one that produces a contradiction under both assumptions.

steps.title: Test option A: 'I will be shot'
steps.formula_raw: An implication X -> Y is false only when X is true and Y is false; in every other case it is true.
steps.apply: Assume the statement is TRUE: prisoner spoke truly, so must be hanged (not shot). But then the statement 'I will be shot' is FALSE — contradiction. Now assume FALSE: prisoner lied, so must be shot. But then the statement 'I will be shot' is TRUE — contradiction again. Both assumptions lead to contradiction. The judge cannot act — must set the prisoner free.
steps.note: This is the liar's paradox applied to punishment.

steps.title: Test option B: 'I will be set free'
steps.formula_raw: An implication X -> Y is false only when X is true and Y is false; in every other case it is true.
steps.apply: If TRUE: prisoner is hanged (not set free) — the statement becomes false. If FALSE: prisoner is shot (not set free) — the statement remains false. In the false case there is no contradiction; the prisoner is simply shot. This is not a paradox. Option B does not force freedom.
steps.note: Asymmetry: the false branch resolves consistently (shot), so no true paradox.

steps.title: Test options C and D: factual statements
steps.formula_raw: An argument is valid iff there is no assignment making all premises true and C false
steps.apply: 'I committed the crime' and 'You committed the crime' are verifiable factual claims. If true, prisoner is hanged. If false, prisoner is shot. Neither creates a self-referential loop. The judge can always apply one of the two punishments.
steps.note: Factual statements have deterministic outcomes under this system.

solution.result: The prisoner says 'I will be shot.' This creates an unresolvable logical paradox under both truth assumptions, leaving the judge with no option but to set the prisoner free. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: The self-referential paradox arises because the statement's truth value depends on the very punishment the statement describes, creating a circular contradiction.

questions.question:
Match the columns.

Column 1         Column 2
1. eradicate     P. misrepresent
2. distort       Q. weak completely
3. saturate      R. use
4. utilize       S. destroy utterly

A. 1S, 2P, 3Q, 4R  B. 1P, 2Q, 3R, 4S  C. 1Q, 2R, 3S, 4P  D. 1S, 2P, 3R, 4Q
questions.to_find: Correct matching of words in Column 1 with their meanings in Column 2

understand.plain: This is a vocabulary matching question. Each word in Column 1 must be matched to its correct definition in Column 2. Knowing precise word meanings is key: eradicate means to destroy completely, distort means to misrepresent or twist, saturate means to soak fully or weaken completely, and utilize means to make use of.

keywords.explain: To completely destroy or eliminate something, especially something harmful.
keywords.example: Eradicate a disease means to wipe it out completely from existence.

keywords.explain: To pull or twist out of shape; in communication, to misrepresent facts.
keywords.example: A tabloid might distort the truth to make a headline more dramatic.

keywords.explain: To soak or fill completely; also used to mean overwhelm or weaken completely by flooding.
keywords.example: Saturate a cloth with water means fill it completely; a saturated market is weakened by overabundance.

understand.visual_alt: A matching table connecting four Column 1 words to their Column 2 definitions.

given.aim: Match each word in Column 1 to its correct synonym or definition in Column 2

terms.meaning: Eradicate comes from Latin 'eradicare' meaning to root out. It means to completely destroy or eliminate.
terms.example: Eradicate polio means destroy the disease completely.

terms.meaning: Distort means to twist the shape or meaning of something; in verbal contexts, to misrepresent.
terms.example: Distort a fact means to present it inaccurately or misleadingly.

terms.meaning: Saturate means to soak to capacity; in context of weakening, a saturated system has been overwhelmed to the point of weakness.
terms.example: A saturated market is one that has been flooded so completely that it weakens.

given.plan: Match by process of elimination using known word origins and meanings: eradicate=S, distort=P, saturate=Q, utilize=R (use). This gives option A: 1S, 2P, 3Q, 4R.

steps.title: Match 'eradicate' to its definition
steps.formula_raw: from P or Q and not P conclude Q
steps.apply: Eradicate = to root out or destroy utterly. Among the options: P=misrepresent, Q=weak completely, R=use, S=destroy utterly. Eradicate -> S (destroy utterly). This rules out S for other words.
steps.note: Latin root 'radix' means root; 'eradicate' = to pull up by the roots = destroy utterly.

steps.title: Match 'distort' to its definition
steps.formula_raw: from P or Q and not P conclude Q
steps.apply: Distort = to twist or misrepresent. Among remaining options (P, Q, R): distort -> P (misrepresent). Eliminates P.
steps.note: Latin 'distorquere' = to twist apart; misrepresent is the closest synonym.

steps.title: Match 'utilize' to its definition
steps.formula_raw: from P or Q and not P conclude Q
steps.apply: Utilize = to make use of something. Among remaining (Q, R): utilize -> R (use). This is straightforward.

steps.title: Match 'saturate' to remaining definition
steps.formula_raw: from P or Q and not P conclude Q
steps.apply: Only Q (weak completely) remains for saturate. Saturate means to fill completely / soak; when something is oversaturated it becomes weak. Saturate -> Q (weak completely).

solution.result: The correct matching is: 1-S (eradicate - destroy utterly), 2-P (distort - misrepresent), 3-Q (saturate - weak completely), 4-R (utilize - use). Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: Process of elimination (disjunctive syllogism) is applied iteratively to match each word to its definition as options are ruled out.
